supporting_evidence: Most common cause of fever and rash in preschool children
Enteroviral, adenoviral, and parvovirus B19 infections account for majority of pediatric fever-rash presentations
Acute onset with concurrent fever is typical viral pattern
Self-limiting condition in immunocompetent children
Classic presentation: fever with sandpaper rash in child aged 4-8
High fever 39.8°C is consistent with GAS infection
Requires rapid antigen detection test
Early antibiotic therapy prevents complications
Common occult source of fever in young children
Female sex increases UTI risk
Rash may be coincidental viral exanthem with concurrent UTI
Urinalysis is a standard part of fever workup in children under 5
Age 4 is within the classic age window
Fever with rash fulfils partial criteria
Requires monitoring for additional features over coming days
Incomplete Kawasaki can present atypically
Must be considered in any child with fever and rash
Non-blanching rash would significantly increase this probability
Lethargy is a concerning feature
Low probability but high consequence if missed

reasoning: Standard pediatric fever and rash differential for a 4-year-old female. Viral exanthem is overwhelmingly the most common cause and ranks highest at 50%. Scarlet fever and UTI are common treatable causes. Kawasaki disease should be monitored if fever persists. Meningococcal disease is listed as a don't-miss despite low probability — clinical rash assessment will be the key differentiator.
